en.extract:
The Bangwa forest warbler or Bangwa scrub warbler (Bradypterus bangwaensis) is a species of Old World warbler in the family Locustellidae.
It is native to the Cameroonian Highlands forests.
It is threatened by habitat loss.
It does not migrate. It is territorial.


Description
It does not exhibit physical sexual dimorphism.


Breeding
It breeds in October and November.


Vocalization
The males' characteristic song consists of repeating the same pitch at increasing volume. Females sometimes duet with them by singing at decreasing pitches. Females are not known to sing solo. 
Bangwa forest warblers sing more frequently in wet than dry season; this difference is especially pronounced in males. The actual amount of precipitation has no effect on amount of singing.
Their peak of vocal activity is during the first hour after sunrise. Males have a second peak of vocal activity 11 hours after sunrise, but females do not.
The function of their vocalizations is unknown.


References

The '''Bangwa forest warbler''' or '''Bangwa scrub warbler''' ('''''Bradypterus bangwaensis''''') is a species of [[Old World warbler]] in the family [[Locustellidae]].
It is native to the [[Cameroonian Highlands forests]].

It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

It does not migrate.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Budka |first=Michał |last2=Uyeme |first2=John Emenike |last3=Osiejuk |first3=Tomasz Stanisław |date=2023-07-14 |title=Females occasionally create duets with males but they never sing solo-year-round singing behaviour in an Afrotropical songbird |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=13 |issue=1 |doi=10.1038/s41598-023-38552-5 |issn=2045-2322|pmc=10349113 }}</ref> It is territorial.<ref name=":0" />

== Description ==
It does not exhibit physical sexual dimorphism.<ref name=":0" />

== Breeding ==
It breeds in October and November.<ref name=":0" />

== Vocalization ==
The males' characteristic song consists of repeating the same pitch at increasing volume.<ref name=":0" /> Females sometimes duet with them by singing at decreasing pitches.<ref name=":0" /> Females are not known to sing solo.<ref name=":0" /> 

Bangwa forest warblers sing more frequently in wet than dry season; this difference is especially pronounced in males.<ref name=":0" /> The actual amount of precipitation has no effect on amount of singing.<ref name=":0" />

Their peak of vocal activity is during the first hour after sunrise.<ref name=":0" /> Males have a second peak of vocal activity 11 hours after sunrise, but females do not.<ref name=":0" />

The function of their vocalizations is unknown.<ref name=":0" />
